Drawing Topics: Multiple Rounding; Chrome

Technische Beschrijving: The Ruton STY 11 is a classic dry iron (without steam) for everyday use. This model is known for its durability. The heavy metal soleplate was a deliberate design choice; the weight of the iron was intended to do most of the work, so the user had to press less hard.

This is an antique brass or copper coal iron with a wooden handle, primarily used in the 19th century. Instead of electricity, the iron was heated by placing live coals in the tray. Openings on the sides provided the necessary airflow to keep the coals burning.
This type of coal iron is very heavy (often between 3 and 5 kilos) because it's made entirely of solid cast iron. It has a chimney at the back to vent the smoke from the glowing coals.

These types of cast iron coal irons were widely used from the mid-19th century until the First World War. The jute wrapping is not a factory standard, but an authentic customization made by the user to hold the metal handle, which could become extremely hot.

The Ruton R10 is an iconic model from the history of Dutch household appliances. This model was the direct successor to the earlier S-series and remained one of the flagship models of the Rudolf Blik factory in The Hague until around 1957.

This is an antique cast-iron charcoal iron that needs to be filled with glowing coals to generate heat. It has air holes on the sides, and the top can be flipped open to fill the charcoal compartment. Such irons were common until about 1950, before electric irons became the norm.

This vintage appliance is an Excelsior vacuum cleaner, a brand with a deep-rooted history in Dutch industry. The suitcase model, or portable vacuum cleaner, was popular because of its compactness and ease of storage, unlike the larger canister vacuums.

This vacuum cleaner is a classic "sled" model with a metal base that allowed it to glide across the floor. This type of vacuum cleaner was often manufactured in Rotterdam during the 1950s and 1960s. Such vacuum cleaners were innovative at the time because they could both suck and blow by connecting the hose to the opposite end.
This is an informational/promotional document for the Siemens Flormatic, a multifunctional suction and brush nozzle (also called a "Saug-Kehr-Bürste") for vacuum cleaners.

In the 1970s, these "tank" models were known for their durability and were often considered appliances that could last a lifetime. The design, with its gray housing, blue head, and red stripe with the logo, is highly characteristic of Philips' visual identity in those days.

This specific model dates from the transition from the classic iron to more modern appliances. Between 1910 and 1930, electric irons were often not yet plugged into an electrical outlet, but instead connected directly to a light bulb socket via a special plug (a "crook").

This is the exact same model travel iron with foldable handle, as sold by more well-known brands like Juliette and Valiant. In the 1960s and 1970s, factories in Japan and Taiwan mass-produced these irons, after which they were sold by various importers under their own names.

This is a vintage Ruton electric iron with a metal frame and electrical connection, typical of mid-20th-century appliances. It has a classic triangular metal soleplate with a dark-colored, ergonomically shaped Bakelite handle.

This iron is considered a classic example of post-war British modernism. It is included in the collection of the Museum of Design in Plastics (MoDiP) for its innovative use of compression-molded Bakelite for the handle. It was one of the first affordable irons with a reliable thermostatic control (the "Auto-Control"), a major advance for households at the time. The CA75 is known for its robustness; many examples are still in working order after 70 years.

This iron is considered a classic example of post-war British modernism. It is included in the collection of the Museum of Design in Plastics (MoDiP) for its innovative use of compression-molded Bakelite for the handle. It was one of the first affordable irons with a reliable thermostatic control (the "Auto-Control"), a major advance for households at the time. The CA75 is known for its robustness; many examples are still in working order after 70 years.

This item is a vintage electric travel iron, also known as a "Piccolo" or produced by brands like Arc or Chesterton. These models were primarily produced between 1930 and 1950. They were popular at a time when electric ironing was becoming modern, but appliances still had to be compact for travelers.

This is an early electric travel iron from the 1920s–1935 period. It's a compact traveler's design with a rattan-wrapped handle for heat insulation. The iron has a 150-watt output and is dual-voltage (110/220V), which was essential for international travel at the time.

This item is a vintage Holland Electro 3000 vacuum cleaner. These vacuum cleaners were known for their robust construction and sold for around 439 guilders at the time. This particular example is a collector's item due to its nostalgic value and iconic retro design.

The item in the photo is a vintage handheld vacuum (also called a dustbuster or table vacuum) from the 1970s. This particular model was popular as an accessory for cars or for small household use on tables and desks. The futuristic, rounded design and bright orange color were typical of the optimistic design style of the 1970s.
This item is a vintage toy iron, also called a miniature iron, that probably dates from the 1950s or 1960s. Such irons were also produced as decorative collectibles.

The Moulinex Variojet Plus 3200 is an iconic steam iron that symbolizes European home appliances of the 1990s. Its design is typical of the brand's functional, ergonomic style from that era, with its signature orange accent buttons.

The Skyline series was known for its sleek, functional design. Its design reflects the transitional period in which household appliances became more compact and were equipped with more modern electronic functions, such as the suction power slider seen on this model.

The Siemens Rapid is an iconic series of handheld vacuum cleaners with a history dating back to the first lightweight models from 1935. In the 1970s, the Rapid line was very popular and appeared in various striking colors that reflected the spirit of the times.
This antique vacuum cleaner is a Siemens-Schuckert Protos boiler vacuum from the early 20th century. The object is made of green enameled iron with a wooden handle.

This specific vintage model is a prime example of Dutch industrial design from its period. Although Philips is now a global concern with production and design centers in various countries, the designs for such classic household appliances came directly from the Dutch design departments at the time.
This specific model stands out due to its Space Age aesthetic, which was very popular at the time. The vertical design was an innovation to save space in smaller households.
This is a vintage Continental electric iron, a collector's item with a distinctive retro design. The appliance features a metal soleplate and a black and white Bakelite housing.

The device has a heavy, chrome-plated or nickel-plated soleplate that retains heat well. The small black knob on the top (often with a scale from 1 to 5 or with plus/minus signs) is used for temperature control.

This object  is a vintage travel iron, a compact and foldable model that was popular in the 1960s and 70s. It is recognizable by the chrome-plated casing and the black Bakelite handle that folds flat for storage.
This specific model was intended as a "mignon" (miniature) travel iron and is today a beloved object among collectors of Italian vintage design.
This model is a vintage iron that was produced by Philips, with advertisements dating back to the 1970s. It was promoted at the time as a lightweight aluminium iron.

This iconic dry iron is known for its typical 1950s and 60s design with a dark brown (bakelite) housing and a chrome-plated soleplate. In 2006, this model was even nominated for the longlist of the best Dutch design of the past hundred years.
The General Electric 3 FD 161 is a vintage electric iron from the 1950s. This specific model is known for its robust mid-century design and was a popular household appliance at the time.

Product (EN): Vacuum cleaner with operating instructions and hose
Technische Beschrijving: This vacuum cleaner in a striking yellow color is now considered a collector's item due to its unique retro design from the 70s/80s.The Deluxe S 220 is an iconic piece of industrial design that is representative of the reliability for which the brand is known.

The design of the Siemens Polymatic (as shown in the photo) is a classic example of functional industrial design from the 1970s and 1980s. During this period, the emphasis at Siemens (part of the BSH Group) was on sustainability, modularity, and ease of use.

The Miele Turbo Team is known for durability and strong suction power. It's equipped with a dust bag system, which is more hygienic when emptying than bagless models and typically features multiple suction settings and special attachments, such as a turbo brush for pet hair.
The Sniffy was a small "stick vacuum" that was very popular in the Netherlands in the 1950s and 1960s. It was a handy model, ideal for stairs, furniture, and small apartments.

Unique to this model is that it can not only vacuum but also blow. This was often used for inflating air mattresses or quickly defrosting freezers. The vacuum cleaner has a power of approximately 750 Watts, which was considered very powerful at the time. Originally, this model came with a braided hose, an aluminum tube, and various brown nozzles, including an automatic combination nozzle for carpets and hard floors.

the Electrolux Cleany is a cordless, rechargeable handheld vacuum cleaner that came with a matching wall mount. In the Netherlands and Belgium, this type of compact, cordless vacuum cleaner is commonly known as a "kruimeldief" ("crumb catcher"). The concept was originally popularized by the Black & Decker "Dustbuster," which came onto the market in 1979.

The design of the AFK AK-2.2 is characteristic of the functional and affordable consumer electronics from the transitional period between the 1990s and 2000s. A key design featureis its streamlined, teardrop-shaped design. The wide suction nozzle flows smoothly into a narrow handle.

This iconic Philips iron, the HD1124, is a prime example of Dutch industrial design from the post-war reconstruction period and the subsequent decades. The design originates from the Philips Corporate Industrial Design Centre (CIDC) in Eindhoven. During that period, this department was led by the renowned Norwegian designer Knut Yran.

This specific model dates from the 1960s or early 1970s. The design with a Bakelite handle and chrome housing is typical of that period. Rowenta's design department at the time was influenced by streamline aesthetics and the functional German design school.

This design reflects the transition from heavy, metal irons to lighter models with plastic (bakelite) handles and a focus on ergonomics and ease of use. The "y" in the model number often referred to a specific regional version or a minor revision in the production process (such as indicator light colour or the wiring), while the basic functionality remained the same as the standard HD 1115.

The HD 2752 series was primarily in production during the late 1970s and the 1980s. It is a classic example of the "heavy" dry irons that were the standard in European households at the time. Philips household appliances from this era were often developed by the in-house design team in Eindhoven.

The model is part of the "Millionaire" or "Popular" series, known for its heavy-duty build, featuring a polished chrome or stainless steel soleplate and a high-impact black plastic handle. During this era, Philips' domestic appliances were heavily influenced by their internal Corporate Industrial Design (CID) group, which prioritized Sense and Simplicity.

The Excelsior 8170 (also known as Signaal) is an iconic vintage vacuum cleaner that was produced around 1976. This specific model is known for its striking vertical design and the typical mustard yellow color that was very popular for household appliances at the time. The device was often supplied with various attachments, including a flexible hose that connects at the top.

This an "all-in-one" device was a unique concept that could stand upright while the hose remained connected. For its time, the suction power (often around 700-1000 Watts) was very high. This blue and white color combination pictured is the most iconic version of the "De Luxe" model.

These objects can serve as excellent subjects for practicing several foundational drawing skills, including: geometric shape analysis; perspective and ellipses;
material and texture rendering.

This FAM canister vacuum cleaner is a rare and characteristic example of post-war European industrial design. The design is a direct result of "Space Age" influences, in which household appliances were designed to resemble products like rockets or jet engines.

Ruton products from the 'RO' line are known for their distinctive red and white color scheme. In the 1960s and 70s, Ruton was a market leader in the Netherlands for appliances such as vacuum cleaners and specialized cleaning machines before the brand name was fully absorbed by Philips.

This specific model is known as the Holland Electro "Colibri". The design was revolutionary because the vacuum cleaner could stand upright, which saved a great deal of storage space at the time in the typically small post-war Dutch homes. Due to its color (hammered green) and design, it is now a highly sought-after collector's item that is even included in museum collections.

The design of the Tefal Turbo Pro 300 is representative of the functional, ergonomic style of the French manufacturer from the early 2000s. It is a steam iron that was positioned at the time as a powerful and user-friendly model for domestic use.

This model is a milestone in American industrial design. The design is closely linked to the influence of the famous industrial designer Brooks Stevens. Stevens designed similar groundbreaking irons for GE and other brands in the 1940s (such as the 'Steam-O-Matic'), which laid the foundation for the 'Classic' aesthetic.

This model was designed as a "Dry Iron," specifically optimized for Permanent Press fabrics that were new to the market at the time. The blue dial on the front has specific settings for synthetic fabrics such as acrylic and nylon. Characteristic is the braided fabric cord with the blue plug, which was typical of GE appliances from that period.

The Azur line was groundbreaking at the time due to the introduction of the 'Drip Stop' system and the ergonomic handle with the characteristic ridges for extra grip. The series won multiple design awards during the 1990s, including the prestigious iF Design Award, for the combination of functional ease of use and a streamlined appearance.

This model was one of the first steam irons to fully automate the process via the 'Steam Control System', reducing the need for manual adjustment by the user. It is now known as an icon of Dutch industrial heritage from that era.
The Type 600z was praised for its handy size and was often sold as the 'ideal helper' for stairs, cars, or small apartments. Today, it is a beloved collector's item for lovers of retro interiors.

This was one of the first canister vacuum cleaners with the characteristic "sled runners" instead of wheels, allowing it to glide easily over carpets. The design is a prime example of the Streamline Moderne style (a later phase of Art Deco), characterized by aerodynamic forms that exuded speed and modernity.

A typical "aerodynamic" 90s design with flowing, organic lines that suggest speed and efficiency. The semi-open handle creates a visual lightness and provides space for thumb operation of the steam buttons. The back is designed to be wide and flat for a stable vertical resting position (heel rest).
The JN1 model was a luxury item at the time, partly due to the precise temperature control required for the then-new synthetic fabrics such as Perlon.

This appliance is a Philips Comfort 200 (model HD 1492), a classic dry iron with a spray function. The  series, including the 200, 300, and 400 models, was primarily on the market in the 1990s (circa 1992–1998).

This is an 'all-purpose' handheld vacuum cleaner. The unique feature of this model was its versatility; by connecting the hose at the top instead of the side, the device transformed from a vacuum into a powerful blower.

This iron was intended as a functional and affordable 'dry' iron (without steam) for domestic use. Today, it is considered a design classic and is popular in the vintage community, not only as decoration but also for specific uses such as waxing skis due to its constant temperature.

The HD 1146 was one of the first models to focus on an ultra-light weight, which was a major selling point for ease of ironing at the time. The housing is made of heat-resistant plastic (often Bakelite or early thermoplastics) with a polished metal soleplate. The design falls under the Modernism of the 1970s, in which functionality and a sleek, geometric design were central.
The Pressing Plus 1 model was sometimes also sold under the brand name Calor. It features a distinctive retro design with a bright orange casing and a chrome soleplate.

Under the leadership of then-chief designer Stefano Marzano, the focus during this period shifted towards 'High Design', where appliances not only had to be technically sound but also comfortable to hold and visually fit into a modern household. The GC1705 was the answer to the demand for a compact, lightweight, and affordable iron that was still powerful enough for daily use.

The design reflects the transition from the angular 70s to the more rounded, ergonomic shapes of the 80s. The iron is equipped with an early version of the Supergliss sole, which was revolutionary at the time due to its smooth gliding ability on various fabrics. It was designed for easy storage, with a notch at the back to neatly wrap the cord around.

This model was introduced in the late 80s when the Braun design team was led by the legendary Dieter Rams. The direct design of the irons is often attributed to his successor Peter Schneider and his team. It follows the philosophy of 'less, but better'. The appliance is a prime example of modernism. Characteristic features include the ergonomic handle, the subdued color scheme (off-white with subtle color accents for the knobs), and the iconic black and white checkered textile cord.

The iron in the image is a vintage steam iron from the brand Rowenta, specifically the model DA49. This specific model was produced in West Germany. It is known as a "steam spray dry" electric iron, which means that it can be used both with and without steam and has a spray function.

This vintage steam iron is suitable for both dry ironing and steam ironing and features an adjustable thermostat and a spray function. It is recognizable by the white housing with a transparent grey/black water reservoir and the distinctive red indicator light on the side.

The design reflects the functional, angular aesthetic of the 1980s with a focus on lightweight use ('Light 'n Easy'). For its time, the SilverStone coating was innovative as an early form of non-stick coating that made ironing synthetic fabrics easier.

The Volta U203 (also sold under brand names such as Electrolux and Elektro Helios) was revolutionary because it was one of the first vacuum cleaners with a fully injection-molded plastic housing. The bright orange housing replaced traditional metal constructions, making the machine lighter and cheaper to produce. Despite its compact size, the unit features an internal cable reel and a mechanical dust indicator. Due to its bold use of color and sleek design, this model is now included in the collections of various design museums, including the Swedish Tekniska Museet.

The 4400 distinguished itself by its vertical 'sleete' design with the characteristic perforated round holes in the housing for the air outlet. It was a powerful model for its time, often equipped with electronic suction power control and indicator lights for the dust bag and filters.

This vacuum could both vacuum and blow, which was an advanced feature at the time for cleaning radiators, for example. The R6 is a classic example of a 'torpedo-model' canister vacuum cleaner, two metal 'sleds' (skis) to glide easily over the carpet. This type of design was very popular in the 1950s due to its futuristic, streamlined appearance.

The packaging in the photo is a classic example of industrial graphic design from this period: The box employs a strict grid, with a mix of horizontal and vertical text placement, giving the packaging a dynamic appearance. Ample white space is combined with the accent color blue (matching the product) and a gold accent for the 'premium' features. The product is depicted in a three-quarter perspective with slight motion blur in the background to suggest action.

This specific model dates back to the 1990s (circa 1992-1998). It was the successor to the more angular models from the 1970s and 1980s.In the world of small household appliances, successful designs were often produced under license or purchased from the same manufacturer by various brands. That is why you still see the exact same design today from brands like Severin.

This model is known for its 'torpedo' design, which is characteristic of the optimism about progress of that era. It is a typical example of streamline design, a style that began in the 1930s and remained popular for household appliances after the war. Due to its unique design, this model is a beloved collector's item and has even been exhibited in museums such as the Kunsthal Rotterdam.

The packaging features the brand name in both the Latin alphabet and Arabic script ("مولينكس"). The design with the pink/red color blocks and the prominent product photo is typical of Moulinex's retail style from that era, focused on ease of use and efficiency.

The appliance offers options for both dry ironing and steam ironing, including a spray function and a second steam program. It features a prominent rotary knob for the thermostat and a visible water reservoir. The colored buttons on the front are for the steam and spray functions.

The Tefal Ultragliss Profil 80 Automatic is a classic steam iron known for its smooth glide and convenient vertical steam function. This function allows you to remove wrinkles fro hanging clothes or curtains by holding the iron vertically and pressing the steam button at intervals.
Tangible model of an iron, for testing the ergonomics (grip) and the visual balance of the housing.

The Miele Electronic S224 features electronic suction power control, a foot pedal for automatic cord rewind, and an indicator that shows when the dust bag is full. This model is beloved by collectors for its distinctive retro orange color and the motor that was very powerful for its time. It was the successor to the S 204 and marked a transition to a more compact and colorful design, with the orange color being typical of the design trends of the late 1970s and early 1980s.

The AEG Rapido AG5106S is a cordless handheld vacuum from the Rapido series, known for its cyclonic airflow and distinctive wheels. This model features the Easy Reach™ nozzle: an integrated, extendable crevice tool so you can easily reach tricky corners without separate attachments.

The Fakir Type 2 was praised for its robustness. At the time, these machines were built to last a lifetime, which explains why many of these examples can still be found in working condition at vintage markets today. The device was part of a broader line of floor maintenance machines that made Fakir (later Nilco) a market leader in Europe.

This specific model, with its cast-aluminum 'shark-nose' triangular base and the illuminated translucent nameplate, was the flagship model of the mid-to-late 1950s. The goal was functional elegance: the heavy aluminum provided the weight needed for polishing, while the triangular shape allowed it to reach into the 90° corners. The translucent blue/purple plate on the front isn't just for branding. In many of these units, there is a small bulb behind it. This was marketed as a luxury feature to help users spot dust and uneven wax patches in dimly lit corners or under furniture.

The Model 700 was a technological milestone and is known for its Art Deco-style industrial aesthetic, featuring a polished aluminum base station, a wooden or steel handle, a woven fabric dust bag and the distinctive triangular logo on the front. This was the first model to utilize the patented 'Positive Agitation' technology, which helped Hoover become the market leader.

This Hoover electric floor polisher is designed for polishing and scrubbing hard floors. The device features a distinctive deep red (crimson) housing made of Bakelite or metal, with two rotating brushes on the underside. This model was very popular in the 1950s and was advertised worldwide. It bears a royal seal ("By appointment to H.M. King George VI"), confirming its British origin and the time period (before 1952). Today, these machines are often considered collector's items or museum pieces due to their mid-century modern aesthetic.

This vacuum has a typical 1980s look with the characteristic beige/off-white color and a focus on ergonomics due to the placement of the controls directly on the housing. The design is modular, making parts such as the cord reel and the motor relatively easy to repair. For its time, the S 125 was innovative because of the steplessly adjustable suction power via the central rotary knob and the ability to use the device as both an upright vacuum and a handheld vacuum (without a stick).

The Sniffy vacuum was revolutionary at the time because it was much lighter and more manageable than the heavy canister vacuums people were accustomed to until then. It was produced at the Holland Electro factory in Rotterdam and came onto the market in the 1950s (approx. 1955), remaining a popular model into the 1960s.

This Hoover Model 262 (also known as the "Hoover Junior" in certain regions) is made of Bakelite and aluminum, which was typical for that time. It features the famous 'Hoover agitator (a beating brush) to vibrate dust out of carpets.

This early Hoover Electric Suction Sweeper is an icon of household history, with a housing made of cast aluminum, which was a modern and luxurious material at the time. The base was designed by James Murray Spangler, a janitor who suffered from asthma and therefore invented an 'electric broom. This model was famous for the slogan "It Beats – as it Sweeps – as it Cleans," thanks to the rotating brush roller that beat the dirt out of the carpet.

The Legend II introduced a new burgundy color scheme and a redesigned 'Mini Emtor' (the plastic part to which the bag is attached) made of durable polycarbonate. The body is largely made of cast aluminum (duraluminium), which contributes to the reputation that these devices last a lifetime. The design focuses on powerful airflow and versatility; the unit can be converted from an upright vacuum to a portable model or even a carpet cleaner.

This is a medium-sized screw-top cup that was originally sold as part of camping or picnic sets, but was also popular as a kitchen storage container. These cups are made of polypropylene, an unbreakable and, at the time, revolutionary type of plastic that was ideal for outdoor use. This type of cup is still a beloved collector's item for lovers of vintage Dutch Design due to its indestructibility and timeless, practical shape.

The bottle is an excellent object for drawing lessons due to its specific geometric and visual properties: it's constructed from a combination of a cube (the bottom part) and a cylinder (the neck/cap), showing how complex objects can be reduced to simple geometric shapes. The handle on the right creates a distinct 'hole' in the shape. In art education, this is a classic exercise to look at the negative space (the shape of the void) rather than the object itself.

The bottle is a classic example of industrial design where form follows function. It's an interesting study object due to the ergonomics (recess functions as an integrated handle);  assymmetrical composition (slanted top helps guide the liquid to the spout); texture and surface (diagonal ridges to increase rigidity to the plastic wall and improve grip); and material description (HDPE with a specific metallic blue sheen).

This is an enameled metal hot water bottle. The rust-brown color with white speckles is also known as 'graniteware' or 'speckled enamel'. This type of finish was popular because it looked less fragile than solid colors and fit well in the kitchens and bedrooms of the time.

The design focuses on sustainability and ease of use. The bottle features a double cap; the small top cap is for drinking, while the larger opening underneath is intended for easy filling and cleaning. The striking silicone band serves as a carrying loop and holds the cap in place so you don't lose it.

Leave-in nourishing hair milk, designed to intensively nourish and repair very dry hair without rinsing. The ball-shaped bottles are part of the repositioning of Fructis in the 2000s. This specific packaging and product line was prominent around 2004-2009.
